document.source_title: Italy rethinks EU defense-financing aid as arms spending falls out of fashion
document.author: Tom Kington
document.summary: Italy is reconsidering its participation in EU defense financing, potentially forgoing €27 billion in rearmament funds as it prioritizes rising energy costs. Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni has indicated that Italy may decline €14.9 billion in SAFE loans from the EU, which are crucial for meeting NATO's defense spending targets. This decision comes amid public discontent regarding arms purchases and a need to address energy security amidst rising costs.
key_takeaways: Italy may forgo €27 billion in defense spending amid rising energy costs.
Prime Minister Meloni is reconsidering the acceptance of €14.9 billion in SAFE loans.
The decision on SAFE loans must be made by the end of May.
Meloni has suggested using the National Escape Clause for energy spending instead.
Public opinion in Italy shows disfavor towards increased arms purchases.
Meloni faces pressure to justify her defense spending decisions to NATO partners.
document.why_it_matters: This situation highlights the tension between defense spending commitments and domestic economic pressures, particularly in the context of rising energy costs. Italy's decisions could impact its NATO obligations and the broader EU defense strategy.

claims.claim_text: Italy may pass on €14.9 billion in SAFE loans from the EU.
claims.evidence_text: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels.

claims.claim_text: Meloni's government is considering using the National Escape Clause for energy spending.
claims.evidence_text: Meloni’s government is now pushing the EU to allow it to use the NEC scheme for a different reason: to park extra spending on soaring energy costs outside the deficit calculation.

claims.claim_text: Public opinion in Italy is against increased arms purchases.
claims.evidence_text: Meloni’s threat to do without defense spending which would flow from the use of the NEC scheme and the SAFE loans — totalling about €27 billion — comes as polling in Italy shows arms purchases are unpopular with voters.

entities.name: Giorgia Meloni
entities.description: Prime Minister of Italy considering changes to defense spending.
entities.evidence_text: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels.

entities.name: Ursula von der Leyen
entities.description: EU Commission chief involved in discussions with Italy regarding defense financing.
entities.evidence_text: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels.

entities.name: Guido Crosetto
entities.description: Italy's defense minister discussing the SAFE loan decision.
entities.evidence_text: Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May.

entities.name: Giancarlo Giorgetti
entities.description: Italy's finance minister whose response is awaited regarding the SAFE loan.
entities.evidence_text: He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response.

events.event: Deadline for Italy to decide on SAFE loans.
events.evidence_text: Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May.
Meloni will nevertheless need to explain to NATO partners why she is considering passing on EU plans to boost defense spending at the next alliance summit in Turkey on July 7-8.

metrics.name: Total potential defense spending foregone
metrics.evidence_text: Italy is considering backing out of a second EU-supported boost to defense spending, taking to about €27 billion the amount of rearmament cash it could forego.
